memory.summary: Quiz is due tomorrow at 09:00, and our 19:00 meeting is still happening—so my day is tightening fast. Assignment A is only half done, and with my low energy and high stress, I’m behind more than I realized.

My energy is crashing and sleep debt is way worse than I accounted for, so the quiz-at-09:00 and the 19:00 meeting both feel like pressure traps. I’m realizing how much stress is shrinking my workable time.

My energy is basically bottoming out and the sleep debt is extreme, so the quiz-at-09:00 and the 19:00 meeting now feel less like options and more like unavoidable pressure. I’m even more behind than I thought.
teammate_A’s chat makes it clear the meeting prep is still hinging on me, but my energy is basically gone and sleep debt is awful—so I’m even more worried I’ll drop the ball than I was earlier.

No new info, but my body’s telling the story: with sleep debt at 6 hours and energy at zero, everything feels like pressure. I’m starting to think I won’t meet expectations unless I somehow break the stress spiral.

I just crossed the fatigue line—my energy is effectively zero and the sleep debt is huge—so any “choices” feel like pressure rather than support, and I’m more scared than motivated about letting people down.

With no new info, I’m still stuck in the same reality check: sleep debt is at 6 hours and my energy is basically dead, so stress feels like the only driver and I’m terrified I’ll disappoint teammate_A by failing to follow through.

Still no new information—just the same blunt pattern: sleep debt at 6 hours and energy at zero make everything feel like pressure. I’m more scared than motivated, and stress is driving my choices, not me.

Nothing new came in, but I’m still pinned: my energy is at zero with ~6 hours of sleep debt, so everything feels like pressure. Stress is calling the shots, and my main worry is disappointing my people.

No new information, but the situation feels unchanged: sleep debt is still ~6 hours, my energy is at zero, and stress is driving everything. I’m most anxious about disappointing teammate_A, not actually choosing what I want.

desires: Avoid being seen as unreliable by teammate_A—my stomach drops at the thought of failing to follow through and letting them down.
Get the stress to shut up for the next few hours by doing the “minimum that counts,” even if I don’t feel like I’m choosing it.
Not crash harder tomorrow—want to protect whatever shred of functioning I have by grabbing sleep instead of forcing more effort when energy is at zero.
Keep some social safety anchor (friends / friend_J) because when I’m this pinned, connection feels like the only counterweight to pressure.

goals.description: 20-minute stabilization reset: water + quick snack, then 10–15 minutes no-scrolling low-stimulation to break the stress spiral.
goals.rationale: When I’m stressed I under-choose and over-commit; this gets my brain back to ‘choices feel possible’ fast.

goals.description: Right after the reset: send teammate_A a short update—acknowledge being swamped, confirm I’m still on track, and give a concrete today return window (even if it’s later than I want).
goals.rationale: Unreliable vibes make my stomach drop; a firm timeline buys trust and quiets my mental noise.

goals.description: Immediately after reset: 45–60 minutes hard quiz prep using active practice, then write a tiny ‘drill again tonight/tomorrow’ list (5–8 items).
goals.rationale: Minimum-that-counts control over the deadline stops the failure/uncertainty spiral.

goals.description: Next 60–90 minutes after the quiz sprint: produce a stop-worthy end-to-end draft for the next hardest section of Assignment A (no polishing).
goals.rationale: Speed-to-safety: getting ‘done enough’ reduces the reputation scramble feeling tomorrow.

goals.description: Before 18:15: set my hard capacity limit for today + pre-write 2 polite deflection lines + 3 genuinely-committable bullets for any later asks.
goals.rationale: I’m vulnerable to yeses; this protects my plan so social energy doesn’t steal full-join time.

goals.description: Cohort_3 warm check-in by 19:30: message that I’m slammed but will catch up soon; keep timelines vague (but not flaky).
goals.rationale: My anchor is connection—staying human reduces pressure when I’m pinned, without hijacking focus.

goals.description: Plan recovery hard tonight: choose a shutdown time + 20–30 minutes low-effort wind-down that doesn’t involve problem-solving, then stop.
goals.rationale: I want to not crash harder tomorrow; sleep beats forcing more effort when energy is at zero.

agent.last_reasoning: Goal g5: I need the reset, but energy is at 0—so I’ll do the closest “next real step” that prevents social spillover: a 2-hour hard drafting push for Assignment A (g4). Side effect weighed: might worsen stress/energy, but staying off scrolling buys momentum over panic.

goals.rationale: I can’t stand the flaking vibe—keeping the dependable social image is my main pressure buffer even when I’m underwater.

goals.description: Start and push Assignment A until it reaches a 'panic-safe' draft: do the next hardest section end-to-end, then stop at a usable version (no perfection spiral). Aim for at least 60–90 minutes uninterrupted.
goals.rationale: It’s half done and deadline-stress triggers me—my move is speed-to-safety, not flawless work.

goals.description: Build a minimal, effective Thu 09:00 quiz prep packet (focused practice + key review): 45–60 minutes now, then make a short checklist for what to review right before bed.
goals.rationale: Sleep debt + stress is the threat; I want tomorrow-morning confidence so I don’t wake up destroyed.

goals.description: Pre-plan how I’ll handle the 19:00 meeting so it doesn’t turn into guilt-driven extra commitments: decide my 'max I can take' beforehand and draft 1–2 polite deflection lines.
goals.rationale: I already feel the trap risk with agreeing to more when I can barely think—so I need guardrails before the social momentum hits.
